New Tansley Review on modeling forest disturbance published in New Phytologist


New Tansley Review argues that mechanistic, scalable modeling of forest disturbance is essential for reducing uncertainty in global change forecasts.

BioM2 website remade with Quarto


The BioM2 Lab website has been rebuilt with Quarto.





Jun 25, 2026












New paper on storm-driven biomass turnover in Amazonian forests published in Nature Climate Change


New paper shows storms are a key driver of spatial heterogeneity in Amazon forest biomass turnover, on top of atmospheric dryness and water stress.





May 3, 2026












Pond mapping paper published in Remote Sensing of Environment


New paper on inventorying ponds with Sentinel-1/2 time series is published in Remote Sensing of Environment.

title: New paper published in Forest Ecology and Management
text: New paper titled “Estimating merchantable and non-merchantable wood volume in slash walls using terrestrial and airborne LiDAR” is now published online in Forest Ecology and Management! This collaborative work, involving Cornell EEB, DNRE, and SUNY ESF, is led by our PhD student Nick Cranmer. The study investigates the woody volume of slash walls, an effective forest management practice to prevent deer herbivory and promote forest regeneration, using terrestrial and airborne lidar systems (TLS and ALS). Key findings include: (1) TLS tends to underestimate total cross-sectional area due to omission of small stem cross-sections (diameter < 5.08 cm); (2) TLS-based wood volume estimates were similar across both short and tall slash walls in the study, with an average volume of 21.34 m³/30 m for short slash walls and 21.50 m³/30 m for tall slash walls, corresponding to 16.9 t dry biomass/30 m and 17.0 t dry biomass/30 m respectively; and (3) average merchantable (diameter > 15.24 cm) and non-merchantable (diameter < 15.24 cm) timber fraction was 68.75% and 31.35% for short slash walls and 65.04% and 34.96% for tall slash walls. This study provides novel data and methodology to estimate the construction cost of slash walls, which is critical for optimizing slash wall applications.

title: NYSDEC site visit at Arnot Forest
text: Arnot Forest sees visits from the New York State Department of Environmental Conservation (NYSDEC) Division of Lands and Forests (DLF) and the Empire State Forest Products Association. During the day trip, lab members Nick Cranmer and Xiangtao Xu gave a field demonstration of terrestrial lidar to help with measuring, monitoring, reporting, and verification (MMRV) of temperate forests, to NYSDEC DLF Director Fiona Watts and other visitors.

title: Welcome baby Elena Rossana Cranmer
text: The Cranmer family has expanded, with baby Elena as its newest member. Congratulations, Nick!

title: Xiangtao Xu gives seminar at Cary Institute
text: Xiangtao Xu gives an invited seminar at Cary Institute of Ecosystem Studies titled “Predicting tropical forest growth and mortality under global change.”

title: BioM2 website remade with Quarto
text: The BioM2 Lab website has been rebuilt with Quarto.

title: First drone lidar flight
text: Nick Cranmer and Xiangtao Xu visited two farms participating in Cornell Cooperative Extension Tompkins County’s Payment for Ecosystem Service (PES) program. The program aims to create a protocol for measuring carbon credit for farmland, where carbon sequestration associated with agroforestry is an important component. On the visit, Nick tested our new WISPR-Livox drone-lidar system (together with curious Grant Xu) and had a successful first drone flight!

title: Cameron passes his A-exam!
text: We are thrilled to announce that Cameron has successfully passed his A Exam! Join us in congratulating Cameron on this outstanding achievement!

title: Coral del Mar presented an oral session on ‘Creating Safe Spaces within ESA’
text: As incoming Chapter Chair for the ESA Latin American and Caribbean Chapter, Coral del Mar presented in a contributed oral session focused on creating safe spaces for early career professionals within ESA.

title: Rong Li presents poster at the Schmidt Science Summit
text: Rong Li, a Schmidt AI4Science postdoctoral fellow at the Cornell University AI for Science Institute, presented a poster titled “Quantifying Neighborhood and Species Interaction Effects on Tropical Tree Growth with Machine Learning and Long-term Census Data” at the 2026 Interdisciplinary Science Summit in North Carolina. The work, co-authored with lab alumni Dylan Van Bramer and Xiangtao Xu, uses XGBoost and graph neural network (GNN) models on long-term census data from the Luquillo Forest Dynamics Plot in Puerto Rico to quantify how neighboring trees and species interactions shape individual tree growth.

title: Our Team
text:
We are an interdisciplinary group working at the interface of ecology, Earth system science, and remote sensing.
Join us! We welcome undergraduate researchers, prospective graduate students, and postdocs. Reach out to Xiangtao if our work resonates with you.

title: Dylan Van Bramer
text: I’m a junior studying Computer Science, with a passion for machine learning and data science, and their applications to ecology. At the Xu Lab, in a collaboration with the Gomes Lab, I am working on machine learning models for forest ecology. Exploring both deep learning and ensemble methods, we are learning about neighborhood interactions between species using forest census data. I am excited to continue learning about both machine learning and ecology throughout my time here.

title: Daniel Petticord
text: I work predominantly at the plant-soil interface. Particularly, I’m interested in how the root and soil “microbiome” influences the interaction between plants and their local environment. My work takes a Tilman-esque approach to plants by thinking of them as consumers of soil resources, and my interests focus around how microbes influence or explain these consumer-resource interactions. Before Cornell, I applied this approach to examine ecosystem-level plant-soil processes that maintain the diversity of tropical tree species. During my PhD, I have transitioned to more applied work. Specifically, my dissertation examines how plant-fungal interactions might play a role in ecologically-damaging nutrient loss from a historically fertilized subtropical pasture. In the future, I hope to bring this approach to study patterns of invasion and succession, particularly in the disturbed “wilderness” of the Anthropocene.

title: Nicholas Cranmer
text: I am primarily interested in using remote sensing technologies such as terrestrial laser scanning (i.e., terrestrial LiDAR) to further understand ecological processes such as carbon sequestration and storage under different forest management practices.

title: Yixin Ma
text: I am broadly interested in how plants interact with environmental variability (both temporally and spatially), and my research centers on phenotypic plasticity, i.e., within-species trait variability driven by different environments. I approach this topic using techniques in ecological modeling and ecophysiology. My current research examines light-driven plasticity within the canopy and how it relates to demography and fitness.

title: Research
text:
“The key to prediction and understanding lies in the elucidation of mechanisms underlying observed patterns. Typically, these mechanisms operate at different scales than those on which the patterns are observed.” — Levin (1992), The Problem of Pattern and Scale in Ecology, Ecology 73(6): 1943–1967
We work at the interface of plant science, ecology, Earth system science, and remote sensing. We aim to improve predictive understanding of our diverse and heterogeneous biosphere, leveraging novel ecological data sets, advanced computational tools, and theoretical analysis. Our research addresses classic questions across ecological scales: To what extent do traits determine plant performance and vital rates? What are the key dimensions of plant functional diversity? How do biodiversity and environmental heterogeneity interact to determine large-scale processes such as carbon and water cycles? We also develop novel quantitative methods to extract ecologically relevant information from multi-platform remote sensing products, particularly terrestrial lidar data.

title: Research
text:
What we do



🌳 Ecosystem modeling
Process-based models (the ED2 lineage and beyond) that scale plant physiology up to ecosystem and biosphere dynamics.




🛰️ Ecological remote sensing
Lidar, drones, and satellite products to characterize vegetation dynamics and land surface processes across spatial and temporal scales.




💻 Model–data integration
A ModEx approach that fuses field experiments, observations, and models to improve predictions and generate new hypotheses about ecosystem dynamics.

title: Research
text:
Research Projects
Current lab research revolves around Tropical forest resilience under environmental change, Forest biomass monitoring and modeling, and Mapping ponds, wetland and their ecosystem functioning supported by NSF, DOE, USDA, NY DEC, NASA, Cornell CALS, and private foundations.

title: CoDiFI
text:
Cornell Digital Forestry Initiative
This project creates a next-generation forestry research hub that provides resources and expertise for integrating novel remote sensing (e.g., drone, lidar) and computational techniques with conventional forestry measurements across a range of departments at Cornell.
(More content coming soon.)

title: Xiangtao Xu
text:
I am a plant and ecosystem ecologist with broad interests in environmental science and Earth System Science. My research revolves around the role of vegetation in our Earth system and searches for quantitative frameworks to understand their ecophysiology and make predictions. I am also interested in novel methods to characterize plant activities across different spatial and temporal scales such as ecological remote sensing.
Currently I teach BIOEE 1610 Introductory Biology: Ecology and the Environment and BIOEE 3550/6550 Ecological and Environmental Data Analytics.

title: Tao Han
text: I am interested in using advanced computer vision techniques and terrestrial laser scanning to understand different ecological processes.

title: Coral del Mar Valle-Rodríguez
text: I am highly interested in using remote sensing techniques to study changes in forest composition and plant behavior due to climate change in the tropics.

title: Cameron Scholl
text: I am interested in the use of remote sensing in identifying the extent and intensity of defoliation, as well as predicting the drivers and long-term impacts of spongy moth outbreaks.

title: Maddie Watson
text: I am an undergraduate student on the Cornell Gymnastics team majoring in environment and sustainability. I am interested in how terrestrial lidar scanners can answer important questions for forest ecology and sustainability.

title: Xiaoting Li
text: I am interested in work that integrates different spatial and temporal scales to understand vegetation phenology-environment interactions. My current research focuses on phenological sensitivity inference with predictive modeling in alpine grasslands on the Tibetan Plateau.

title: Donghai Wu
text: I focus on providing insights into three scientific questions: (1) How to quantify the destinies of the carbon atoms fixed by photosynthesis? (2) How does the ecosystem respond to environmental change? (3) How to establish a positive feedback between human and nature?

title: Sabrina Chow
text: Leaf lifespan, ecology, conservation.

title: Welcome baby Charlotte Nan Scholl
text: Congratulations, Cameron!

title: Xiangtao Xu gives keynote presentation at ER-DGVM-C
text: Xiangtao Xu gives a keynote presentation titled “Trait-based modeling of tropical forest structure and dynamics: critical dimensions of plant functional diversity,” kickstarting the two-and-a-half-day East Regional Dynamic Global Vegetation Modeling Conference (ER-DGVM-C) at MBL, Woods Hole. Lab members Cameron Scholl, Coral Valle-Rodriguez, and Tao Han also presented research talks and posters. Tao Han has won the best postdoc poster award!

title: Inaugural CoDiFI summer training
text: The inaugural summer training course of the BioM2-led Cornell Digital Forestry Initiative (CoDiFI) hosts a mix of PhD students, professional Master’s students, and foresters at Arnot Forest. The 4-day workshop introduces the foundations of lidar remote sensing (talk given by Xiangtao Xu), procedures of applying tripod-based and backpack lidar systems (demo given by Nick Cranmer, Yixin Ma, and Tao Han), and processes of conducting RGB drone flights (demo given by Hannah Zonnevylle from the Katz Lab).

title: New paper published in Global Change Biology
text: New paper titled “Two sub-annual timescales and coupling modes for terrestrial water and carbon cycles” is now published online in Global Change Biology. Dr. Dan Gianotti from MIT CEE is the lead author while Xiangtao Xu of the BioM2 Lab is a co-author. Using remote sensing data, the study identifies two dominant modes of terrestrial water and carbon cycles across the contiguous United States: (1) a negative-correlation timescale on the order of a few days, during which landscapes dry after precipitation and plants increase their carbon uptake through photosynthetic upregulation, and (2) a slow, seasonal-scale positive covariation through which landscape drying leads to decreased growth and carbon uptake. The spatial pattern of local temporal dynamics — positively sloped tangent lines to a convex long-term mean-state curve — is surprisingly strong and can serve as a benchmark for coupled Earth System Models. The study shows that many such models do not represent this emergent mean-state pattern, and hypothesizes that this may be due to a lack of water-carbon feedbacks at daily scales.

title: A new doctor in the house
text: Yixin Ma, the very first graduate student in BioM2 Lab, gave a wonderful public defense today! Congratulations Yixin and wish you the best for your upcoming journey at Harvard University as a post-doc!

title: Pond mapping paper published in Remote Sensing of Environment
text: New lab paper titled “Inventorying ponds through novel size-adaptive object mapping using Sentinel-1/2 time series” is now published online in Remote Sensing of Environment! The project is a collaboration with the Holgerson Lab at Cornell EEB and led by visiting student Denghong Liu from Hong Kong Polytechnic University. The study develops a novel object mapper (OptiSAR-POM) for individual ponds down to ~0.2 hectare in size. It has high accuracy and scalability across diverse landscapes and will contribute to better inventorying of small water bodies across New York State, which are biogeochemical hotspots for greenhouse gas emissions.

title: New Tansley Review on modeling forest disturbance published in New Phytologist
text: New Tansley Review titled “Mechanisms and scales in modeling forest responses to changing disturbance regimes” is now published online in New Phytologist. The review synthesizes the mechanisms underlying vegetation responses to disturbance across spatio-temporal scales — from disturbance occurrence and immediate structural damage to post-disturbance community reorganization and long-term ecosystem feedbacks — and identifies key challenges for representing these processes in terrestrial biosphere models. The key message: advancing mechanistic, scalable, and observation-constrained modeling of disturbance across scales is essential for reducing uncertainties in forecasting global change impacts on ecosystems and the carbon cycle. Several BioM2 Lab members contributed to the review, including Xiangtao Xu, Cameron Scholl, Yixin Ma, and Tao Han.

title: New paper on storm-driven biomass turnover in Amazonian forests published in Nature Climate Change
text: New paper titled “Increasing atmospheric dryness and storms accelerates biomass turnover in Amazonian forests” is now published online in Nature Climate Change. Led by lab alumnus Donghai Wu, the study demonstrates that storms are an important driver of spatial heterogeneity in Amazon forest biomass turnover rates, on top of the effects of atmospheric dryness and water stress. The findings highlight that disturbance modeling frameworks need to explicitly represent storm impacts, not just drought stress, to accurately capture patterns of biomass turnover across the Amazon. Yixin Ma and Xiangtao Xu are co-authors on the study.

title: Welcome Aaron Sams
text: Dr. Aaron Sams has officially started as a part-time programmer in the BioM2 Lab. Aaron was previously affiliated with Cornell MBG before he worked at Embark Inc. He will work with the team to develop the Forest Lidar Package in Python (FLiP). Welcome Aaron!
